meta.title: Defense in Depth: Building a Security Layer
meta.excerpt: Moving from point fixes to a repeatable security boundary

props.text: Security improvements were being added ad hoc: a CSP header here, a validation check there. Each fix solved a specific issue, but we had no consistent security boundary across the app. The result was uneven protection and duplicated logic.

props.title: Fragmented Security
props.body: Point fixes leave gaps. Without a shared layer, teams re-implement checks and forget edge cases in new routes.

props.text: We had inconsistent authentication checks, no rate limiting for public APIs, and scattered input sanitation. High-traffic endpoints were the most vulnerable because each team implemented protections differently.

We created a security layer that runs before every request: authentication, authorization, input sanitization, rate limiting, and structured audit logging. It became the default entry point for all routes.

steps.title: Authenticate
steps.description: Validate session or API token before processing.

steps.title: Authorize
steps.description: Check permissions against role and resource scope.

steps.title: Sanitize
steps.description: Normalize and sanitize input to prevent injection.

steps.title: Rate Limit
steps.description: Throttle abusive traffic and protect critical APIs.

steps.title: Audit
steps.description: Log structured events for incident review and analytics.

features.title: Default Secure
features.description: Make the secure path the default so new routes inherit protections automatically.

features.title: Layered Checks
features.description: Authentication and authorization are separate concerns. Treat them as distinct steps.

features.title: Reusable Policies
features.description: Model rate limits and scopes as reusable policies, not bespoke logic.

features.title: Audit Everything
features.description: Structured logs make incident response measurable and repeatable.

props.title: Key Takeaway
props.body: Centralize security in a reusable layer. When every route passes through the same pipeline, coverage is complete and maintenance drops dramatically.
